summary: The paper presents SAMT, a two-stage framework that generates both the geometry and the texture of a 3D facial avatar from a single input image. In the first stage, an image-conditioned latent 3D diffusion model (a DiT operating over a DoraVAE latent) is pretrained and then specialized to the face domain via large-scale fine-tuning on a curated corpus of roughly 35K head/avatar meshes, producing detailed facial geometry. In the second stage, the generated mesh is textured by a geometry-conditioned 2D texturing diffusion that is guided by multi-view facial priors synthesized from the input image and coupled with a derivative-free mesh-image alignment step, so that the resulting texture is mesh-aligned and consistent across viewpoints. As a byproduct, the authors assemble and preprocess three sources into the 35K-mesh training set. Experiments against recent general-purpose 3D generators and texturing methods report improvements in facial geometry structure and in texture detail and cross-view consistency, and ablations examine the LoRA adaptation, the mesh-image alignment, and the multi-view prior.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is clearly written and easy to follow, with well-organized stage-wise figures and a straightforward articulation of the pipeline and the mesh-image alignment objective. It targets an impactful and genuinely difficult problem: turning a single image into a high-quality, re-renderable 3D face asset that has both structurally coherent geometry and view-consistent texture. On the geometry side, specializing an image-conditioned latent 3D diffusion backbone (DiT over a DoraVAE latent, with sharp-edge sampling and dual cross-attention to preserve facial micro-structures) to the face domain through large-scale fine-tuning on a curated 35K-mesh corpus is a sensible design, and the assembled dataset is itself a useful resource for future work. On the texture side, injecting multi-view facial priors into a geometry-conditioned 2D texturing diffusion, together with a derivative-free alignment step, is a well-motivated way to attack the cross-view inconsistency that commonly plagues single-image reconstruction, and the qualitative results are convincing in difficult regions such as hair, eyes, and profile views. The ablations over the LoRA adaptation, the alignment, and the multi-view prior lend support to the design choices, so the work is technically sound at the level of its individual components.

That said, the originality is limited. Most of the building blocks — latent 3D diffusion with a DiT, the DoraVAE latent, LoRA fine-tuning, geometry-conditioned 2D texturing diffusion, and multi-view rendering priors — are adopted from existing lines of work, and the contribution reads more as a careful domain specialization and integration of these components than as a fundamentally new modeling principle. This is compounded by a heavy reliance on external pretrained models (a DINOv2 image encoder, a pretrained multi-view face generator, and the geometry-conditioned texturing diffusion), which makes it hard to attribute the reported gains to the proposed contributions rather than to the capacity of the foundation models. The latter concern is aggravated by the fact that the pretrained texturing diffusion is underspecified: its architecture, training data, resolution, and conditioning pathways are not described in enough detail to reproduce the system or to assess what SAMT adds on top of it.

The evaluation also has gaps that weaken the significance of the claims. The baselines are general-purpose object generators (e.g., Trellis2, Hunyuan), while comparisons against avatar- and face-specific single-image reconstruction and generation methods — including optimization/SDS-based and feed-forward approaches, and closely related face/UV texturing pipelines such as AvatarTex — are neither included nor discussed, even though these are the most natural competitors. The geometry evaluation leans on non-standard cross-modal proxy metrics (ULIP/Uni3D) alongside a small-scale Chamfer-distance comparison, and it uses an LLM to generate the textual descriptions that drive the retrieval metrics, which hurts reproducibility and could bias the numbers. The texture-consistency improvement is shown only qualitatively (Fig. 8) without a corresponding quantitative result, and the ablation would be more compelling if it also isolated the phase-one model without the face-domain fine-tuning. There are also a couple of specific presentation issues: the alignment optimizes only global scale and translation while omitting rotation, which could be fragile when the canonical orientation is imperfect; and the repeated claims of "topology-regular" and "production-grade" meshes sit uneasily with the stated Marching Cubes extraction, which typically yields irregular triangle soup rather than production-ready quad topology, so these claims should be tempered unless an unmentioned retopology step is involved. Finally, computational cost (training/inference time, hardware) is not reported, and there is no dedicated discussion of failure cases.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Can you fully specify the pretrained geometry-conditioned 2D texturing diffusion used in Stage 2 — its architecture, training data, resolution, conditioning pathways, and multi-view attention implementation — so that the results are reproducible and the contribution of SAMT can be separated from that of the foundation texturer?
2. What is the main advantage of SAMT over avatar/face-specific single-image methods, i.e., single-image 3D face reconstruction and generation approaches (optimization/SDS-based as well as feed-forward)? Why are these not compared against or discussed, given that the current baselines are general-purpose object generators?
3. Why is the mesh-image alignment restricted to global scale and translation? Did you experiment with rotation (or at least yaw-only rotation), and how did it affect cross-view consistency and identity preservation?
4. Please clarify the "topology-regular" and "production-grade" claims in light of the Marching Cubes extraction. Is there a retopology post-processing step that was omitted from the text, and if not, would you consider softening these claims?
5. What is the full-pipeline inference time (mesh generation, alignment optimization, and texture synthesis) and the hardware used, and how robust is the method to challenging inputs such as heavy occlusions, accessories (glasses, hats), extreme hairstyles, and strong directional lighting?
limitations: The limitations discussion is insufficient. The paper omits a dedicated analysis of failure cases (e.g., extreme lighting, heavy occlusions, accessories, or unusual hairstyles, where outputs can look cartoonish relative to real scans) and does not report computational cost, and the strong "production-grade/topology-regular" claims are not reconciled with the Marching Cubes mesh extraction. Adding a failure-case paragraph with visual examples, reporting runtime/hardware, and tempering these claims would make the discussion adequate.
